speaker: interviewer
text: You survived a very close shave. David, where do we go from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking now-- we can now talk 1944. Because during the time of the Christian side-- we'd been here and there. We got good contacts in the underground Polish organization. They were help them in some ways by doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: What month are we talking, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? We're talking from the beginning till the Polish resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we'd been living practically a normal life. We didn't have any problems-- many problems there. We did have good people. Very honest people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We did have monies from previous which we left there. We left fur coats, which my wife did have a fur business. And I-- I smuggled them out during the war, during the years of the ghetto, smuggled out the fur.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I left it with [INAUDIBLE] friend--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and a Polish ship. And he supplied us with money every month very honestly, very nicely, really nicely and very honest. And we did have good contacts then till the uprising came, the Polish uprising. I joined first the AL or whatever it is. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: The- the AL. The Polish resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the socialist resistance, AL. Because that was not far from the state I was living. So I first to the first one. I wouldn't care less-- against the Germans, I don't care with whom I fight. Then in the-- in the middle of it-- about five times or six times. Later, I stopped there. And I went to the place where we started to build ourselves bunkers. In case something will happen, we got to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: This place was [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. That was two streets, which we--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The two streets which we built one entrance and one-- one outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So an entrance and an escape if you needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in contact with these 20 Jews all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the 20 in the beginning. We did have about 10 or 12, yes. But the rest came in during the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they-- already-- and they bombed and-- and old houses which are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in normal ones, but an old one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Pole-- the Poles bombed or the Germans bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans bombed. The Germans [INAUDIBLE] bombing. During the resistance-- the Polish resistance, Jewish resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us the dates of the Polish resistance, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was on the 1st of September, if I am not mistaken was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: September. And when we start-- when we-- when we got to the build, we already start in the night time hiding a little bit outside the places where we'd been before. My wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by hiding outside the places where you were before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside where we'd be in private places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you hiding on the outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the outside? Because we're being Jews. So we felt safer in the bunkers than turning around and going around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's-- we're still between the Poles. And something can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started at night being in the bunkers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to be in the bunkers some nights, some outside it. Depend what we got work to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we'd been already in the underground, in the Polish underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was their-- what you call it-- a guard for a hospital. [INAUDIBLE] and something else. That was a very old hospital there--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say we, does that mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife used to come to her-- to her brother-in-law-like. I am not her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no good to be husband. I'm dark, and she's growing already without [INAUDIBLE]. So that's no good. And we still had been building. And we built up this when the resistance fall when I did have to walk out of Warsaw, the 800,000 people. Or, nearly 800,000 people, because they finished the resistance. The Germans told them to surrender. And they did surrender. I think Bor-Komorowski was the general that surrendered. And I start to walk out of Warsaw. We'd been staying next to the walking out people with the red and white band. And we used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the red and white signify?

speaker: interviewee
text: Red and white, it's the Polish flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: --resistance fighter, but Christian. Polish. And I used to say to the boys, drop the gun. Because I did have to leave the gun further down for the Germans. Guns, grenades-- I said, if I seen a good gun, a machine gun, I said, drop the gun here. Leave it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left it with us, because what I thought is one important thing. If someone is staying there, maybe they're going to rise up again. Because they haven't been informed what they got to do. The information was only one. Leave it with the Germans. But if a Pole is taking it, give it better to a Pole. That I haven't been advised even about it. But from themselves thinking that someone is standing there, give him the gun. And we used to take the gun and brought down to the bunkers. We been very well-- we been very well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was Luba in the bunker with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luba was with me. And this, again, is a very important point. We're going to the bunker. The resistance finished. The Poles going out of Warsaw. 305 people stayed in Warsaw, Jews, hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: In different bankers in different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- when-- what days or months are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about the end of the resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of the Polish resistance. And when we went to the bunkers, we start to organize all the bunkers around in the night time. You are in a bunker. Don't-- we don't want to know the address. But give us the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the name of the bunker? Sigmund. Good. Sigmund, remember that you got such and such law if you go in the night time. We go to each other to know who is going-- Password.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so we got the signal, a password, so we know who, it's not the Germans in there. And we are not allowed to say the word halt-- that means stop. Because for the name for the word halt, we're dropping everything we got, what we took out from the cellars for our bunker. We're dropping it to run away. So we may run away and drop the things. That mean you're going to rob us. That penalty for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a common password? Or, did every bunker have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we knew-- we knew only Sigmund, or [MAKES CLICKING SOUND].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did it-- we'd been living in a good environment, whatever, there. And now I want to finish with it by how we're being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, we being men which knew where our bunker is came at the day when everybody was already-- that's on the 17 or 18-- on the 17 of January when the Polish army moved into Warsaw. Because three months I been standing out. And we been three months and few days down in the bunkers--

speaker: interviewee
text: We been like free from-- but not yet. We are will in the bunkers. And a friend of ours is coming in and said to us, open the door, you are free. We said, no, thank you. We're not opening.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say it was a friend of yours. How did you know it was a friend of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend he knew. He knew. That particular one knew about our entrance. He was so close to us that he knew we knew about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Jewish friend or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish, a Jewish friend. No Poles there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you say, no, thank you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank you. We won't go out of there. Because we knew-- we're being sure that the German is staying behind him with a gun and telling him to open for us to kill us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And now that's what I told you before. And now we being sure that to be free mean that was still alive. So my wife told me one thing. Please, David, I beg you. Shoot me. When she was already free, not believing in it, shoot me. Because I don't want to fall to the Germans again. She was sure that the German is in back of this man. And kill me, kill me. I said, don't be silly, wait. We'll see how that goes. And after a day or so, he came to us for two days begging us to open it. Then he said something. He said open the door-- the edge-- whatever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I will put my legs in. If you think that a German is here, pull me in and shoot me. We did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You pulled him in and shot him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. After three days, we decide to do the exercise. We took him from the back of the entrance. He put in his legs, and we pulled him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he start to laugh. He said, now, wait for the German to come in. Where is the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a very brave man. And he went out-- not out, but with his head looking around. He said, no Germans, nobody there. So we are free. To make something clear, how we thinking of being free, is two days before we send out a Jewish couple, a men-- two men-- two people to up about four, five stories. And we said to them to watch our around-- what is around us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we heard-- yeah, we heard Russian songs. So the Russian is the Russians which work with the Germans. That's [INAUDIBLE]. So we asked-- we asked the boys to go in to-- we drew--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I lost. And I went out to [INAUDIBLE]. And I went out together to watch them. And I came back with a story. As this is 3 kilometers the front between the Germans and the Russians, if you got anti-aircraft guns, you're putting a flag of your nation next to the-- to the anti-aircraft.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the planes are running such a short distance, that I can kill your own people. But how do you know that the enemy won't see it? They won't. Because the Russians or the Germans know quite well the sound of the plane which is their's and which is the Russian's, They can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So if I see that the Germans are running, I taking away their flag and starting to shoot at the planes. Because this is called [NON-ENGLISH]. If it's very short, you've got to put the flag. And when the boys came back, they said to me, we are in trouble. A red flag around [INAUDIBLE] with [NON-ENGLISH] in the center. We got the Germans in our yard. Watch out in the night time. Don't talk too much. Don't cook. Nothing. We're going to be shut up for the few days--

speaker: interviewee
text: --till the guns goes away. What was it there? That wasn't a [NON-ENGLISH]. But it was a hammer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sickle. I did look at it. And I been so taken with Hitler, with the war, with everything-- the [? dying ?] [INAUDIBLE] of seeing something, I must probably looked out. As soon as they seen the flag and this, I got panicked. And I--

speaker: interviewee
text: --paralyzed. And I looked away. And I went down to come back to our bunker. Not even [INAUDIBLE]. Because they didn't know what we knew, that free mean only one thing, dead to be free. Otherwise, a person is not free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, what date-- what date did you walk out of the bunker?

speaker: interviewer
text: So walking out, breathing free air-- what direction did your life take? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I left all in the bunker. I washed myself the first time with water. Because everybody got only a glass of water. And I remember the smell in the thing there with one glass of water to wash your teeth, to wash yourself with the glass, and to drink the glass of water, stinking water, not even clean water. And they did give me to clean myself and to dress a little bit better. And I went to Prague across the Vistula to advise the Jewish committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Jewish committee, but the Jews which will be there already free.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Russian-- in the Russian ends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got there through a bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked, yes. That's not hard. Because that was a [INAUDIBLE] bridge-- a bridge made of timber by the Russians to transport the- the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's a whatever, with the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you choose Prague to go to? That was you-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prague was the nearest one. My brother-in-law was there. And that was the nearest part of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was you and Luba?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Luba stays with the boys there. I will bring the help.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will bring the-- the help to them to get them out and to take them to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. You-- not everybody went out from the bunker? They still stayed below?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We hold them in the bunker. I said, you sleep in the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they still stay in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nowhere to stay. Warsaw was burned out anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there we did have where to sleep, and we did what to eat. So we-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So having been freed and being sure that it was free, did you lock the bunker at night or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. Because we didn't have to lock the bunker at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did it take you to get to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Prague about a day or one-- no, not a day, a few hours. And then I stay in Prague about two days in Prague, two days. And then I came back and told them, all right, we can go. There's a committee. There's everything. Let's pack up. But in the meantime, we did have a few problems with the Russians. They did want vodka, we didn't have it. They said that they will shoot us, a few little things which are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you pack up with to go back and bring the others out of the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- no, I checked the committee there. That's a Jewish committee--

speaker: interviewee
text: --already formed for months. And I took him away and said where the committee is, 47 [INAUDIBLE] or 49. 47 even [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them go there. And then I will settle you and tell you who is left, who is not left, and give you some money. Because they got some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, I bought food. And the people will most probably wonder, how did we get food for three months and some days in the bunkers? Very easy. Very easy. We did have tons of food. Because if every one of the Poles which left-- 800 people-- left 10--

speaker: interviewee
text: --10 [INAUDIBLE]-- 800,000-- 10 [INAUDIBLE] of food buried in the cellars. Because everybody was living the last three months in the cellars, because of the bombing. We took the food downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you found only 10 [INAUDIBLE] from each one, we've got tons of food. And we found the food. So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, when you came back to the bunker, what-- how did you proceed? How did everybody come out? Where they registered, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we haven't been registered. We went to the-- to Prague-- to Praga. And in Praga--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a track to bring--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] No, no, no, everybody walked. We didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't send us a track, nothing. Everybody walked. I took Luba, my wife, and went out. And everybody went out on his own. Because he got an address-- an address where to go and how to go. So they went-- I told them at the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So arriving in Prague, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arriving in Prague, after two, three months, I was arrested--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I belonged to a Polish organization. And that's I'm a Christian. When I like to prove that I'm Jewish, he said, if you will try again, I'll take you downstairs and kill you like a dog. I didn't know Russian at all. I will be like Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was screaming I am Jewish, because I thought that Stalin will come. And he will kill the man, which is such a big hero like David Landau. Jan [INAUDIBLE] is going to tell him what to do and how to do and arrest him. Stalin will kill that officer. What is he doing with such a big hero like David Landau-- like me?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I been-- there I said what do you want from me? I'm a Jew and a Jew, and a Jew. And then I thought myself-- what do you want, actually? He said, what I want from you is to give me names and addresses of your leaders.

speaker: interviewee
text: --In a Polish uniform. [NON-ENGLISH] That means, I am a Pole from Baku, from Russia, but honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of your leaders of what? Of ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: And to give him the names, which I knew a few, but [INAUDIBLE]. But I said, I am not a Pole, so I don't know. I am a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, he didn't find my photo as a German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had been dressed as a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: --[INAUDIBLE] during mine uprising. Don't you know that? Yeah. I'll show you in a minute in the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Israeli museum is the photo, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll show you when you start eating. And I didn't have the photo, because I did give it as a souvenir to my cousin--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which came from Russia. And he was forcing me to tell him something. And I thought myself, all right. If I got to help him, I got to help him. I said, let me out. Let me relax a little bit, because it's after the war. I been a Jew hidden in there. I told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been hiding. Let me rest a little bit. I'll come back. And I will tell you everything, but let me rest. But I give you at the moment all the most important people, the leaders. I am Jan [INAUDIBLE]. He doesn't know Landau at all. So I said to him, one was Miriam Landau. She was the leader, the partisan. Then was Israel Landau.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew they were both dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the leaders in the Polish partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ludwig Landau was a leader in the young groups. And then was [INAUDIBLE] and was [INAUDIBLE]. I knew that all are dead. No one is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I give him my family as the leaders of the Polish underground. But I'll give him much more when I rest. And we made an agreement that he will send me people there. He'll tell me everything, where to go and what to do and when to come back and let me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they grabbed me-- when they arrest me, they arrest me with a revolver. But I been allowed to carry a revolver. Because everyone which was fighting against the fascists could have a revolver. We being there in the underground in the bunkers, we've been fighting against the Germans. We don't have a lot of ammunition. I told them where it is. Everything. And he said-- he got the revolver. And that was all right. He said, when you come back, you'll get back the revolver. You document Jan [INAUDIBLE], all the papers which you got, everything you will get back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they freed me at the moment. I was freed by the Russians from my boots, from my jacket, from my belt, from my shoes. I went home 3 kilometers in a-- in a cold frosty day in the-- Socks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sock, nothing else. 3 kilometers away. And when I came home, I said to [? Lee ?], we've got to hide quick and smart. And I like to run from here. I don't want to-- I went to the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: --agency and told them about my happening. They suggest-- the woman which was running it is here. She's the mother of the Israeli ambassador in Poland. She's from Poland. The ambassador is a Polish boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name? It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give you the name in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Luba, she'll give you the name. She-- she was there. And they said if I work with the brigade--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for a year, they'll smuggle out the family, me, my wife, my cousin, the little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Naomi. And I will do it everything for me. If I know a language? Yes, I know foreign language. That was Czechoslovakian. I knew it before the war. And they help us to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went into hiding until they were ready to smuggle you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: To hiding to take out and to start working.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me out-- few months I was there. They took me out [? and I take you to. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: To the border. They took them to a camp Erding near Monachium for the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then for me, I was working on the border of Czechoslovakian German border. And that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a place where they used to do the best-- the best German [INAUDIBLE]. The-- the-- beautiful--

speaker: interviewee
text: Figurines. The best one in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the best. My wife will remember better I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I been working there only nine months. I made a bargain with them--

speaker: interviewer
text: You made the bargain with the brigade.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the brigade, yes, only nine months. I got a wife. I used to come for two weeks or one week to the camp there. [INAUDIBLE] to make me a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Luba was in a-- in a displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With my brother-in-law and sister-in-law and with the little girl which was taken away from Germany [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the nine months, I came to Germany. And I did look for a place where to go. Now, I did want to go to do the moon. Mine idea was with my wife to go to the moon, because that was the dream, that this is a very far away place from Europe. I couldn't find any further than the moon at that time, but unfortunately I couldn't go to the moon. So I choose Australia as the furthest, the very far away place. And we did have here somebody which send us some papers. We could go to Mexico easily. Or, to my auntie in the United States I could go. But that wasn't far enough for me. Australia was the furthest. I decided actually I did want to go to Palestine, but my wife will tell you when she start to talk to you about it. But I went to-- after a lot of talk-- to

speaker: interviewee
text: --as a [INAUDIBLE] and brought to the-- to the-- I got the photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you arrested in France?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they put you away or release you immediately?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put you away or release you immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They released me not immediately. They took me to the English council. Not general, but council. Because that was not Paris. That was already-- I think [INAUDIBLE] Paris-- they took me to council, interview me. They asked me questions. Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] You worked here, you worked there. I said no, no, no, no. But when our boat came to Australia, I was-- I was working on the boat. [? John ?] [? de Witt. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was a worker there. I was working, getting 50 pounds for the work, and special food. First class food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you doing on the boat?

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave-- you left from Marseilles. When did you leave, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '46 I left. '46 I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came straight to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We went-- first, we went from Poland to Germany, from Germany to France, and from France to Australia. And in-- there in France-- [INAUDIBLE]. But the boat which went through Suez Canal-- when they hit, before they hit Suez Canal, they took five boys. I don't know the reason the other boys, young boys. And they locked us out downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Suez. They thought that we'll jump Suez Canal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Fine. So then you got to Australia. You landed in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne. I landed in Sydney and then came to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the boat you came on?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? John ?] [? de ?] [? Witt. ?] OK. And why, when you landed in Sydney, did you come to Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I did have the people here in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since your time, do you remember the date you landed in Australia in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think February 1947 if I am not mistaken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed in Sydney, or you came straight to Melbourne?

speaker: interviewer
text: And Luba and you and your brother-in-law and sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were here. So having come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Having come to Australia, what did you do? How did-- how did your life start here? Did you-- did you get assisted by the Jewish agency? Or, did you start working on a job? How did your life go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't been on a job. I start a little bit in leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tanning. Not tanning, exactly, but finishing. The finishing of the leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working for yourself or for somebody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? I been with my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the leather already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was in fur. He wasn't in leather. He was in fur business. And then we start off little fur business. Small, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Little-- not furrier. We did specialized things--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from little pieces, we made beautiful coats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother-in-law opened a clothing factory. I was a partner to it, quiet partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the furrier's shop, business?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was only a business. Goodrich and Landau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another one, their clothing was Leading Fashions--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] in Italy, bim, boom, bim, boom, bim, boom. Our Australian machine, rrrr, too quick. Me finish. So I thought that she wants to finish completely. She didn't want to finish. She want to finish by hand. Oh, my daughter is a very good cutter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which factory did she work? No factory. She cut sandwich every morning for the husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, you're pulling our leg. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not. I am not. That's how it was at the clothing factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, so then from the clothing factory, where did your life go?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit in a tannery, and then to building. We'd been partners to a tannery which been losing 10,000 pounds a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two [INAUDIBLE] the company, and they explained us that the losing of the money will take about 10 years. But then we'll start to gain profits. So I said to my partner, which was at that time, I said look, my dear friend, I am not going to stay in a place where we're losing 10,000 pounds. I have got 10,000 shillings. So you're talking about 10,000 pounds. And we ran out of the factory. That was Mr. [? Rubinstein ?] run the factory there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one which run at that time Jewish news.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give you-- here, I can give you names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then you went into building. Did Luba in the early stages work together with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you have a child. When was your child born?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you only have one child.

speaker: interviewee
text: If she wouldn't wait for me-- no, yes, one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewer
text: David, we've covered a lot of territory. I think it's unbelievable that you survived the whole war in Warsaw. You're here. Now, you-- you are definitely living, because you're here to tell us the tale. Thank you for sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but before we finish, we-- only one word. The whole story I told you-- it's-- you won't offend me at all, if listening to the story, you won't believe in it. Because I don't believe myself. With that, I like to finish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can understand that, David. Thank you for sharing your story with the visual Shoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are privileged to have listened to your story.
